Morning.
Yeah, please. Thank you.
Well, good morning and welcome to the
Social Services Community League Committee.
Uh, thank you for coming in this morning, bright
and new on uh,
what's gonna be the pumps last thing day today.
So good. Pardon? An important, an important topic.
Um, got a briefing from you on population this morning.
We got to 30 minutes, so look, um, in your hands
and take it away.
Alright, well,
uh, I'm Jane Wrightson, the retirement commissioner,
and this is my colleague, Dr. Michelle Reyes,
the policy lead at the commission.
Um, thank you for inviting us
to brief you this morning on the long term outlet
for New Zealand's population.
Um, at this time of year.
I certainly feel I'm aging fast,
and I'm sure you do too. Indeed,
We are for this year.
So let's talk about how it's,
what it's like for all of us, shall we?
Um, you, as you probably know, the role of the commission is
to provide independent, uh, evidence-based advice
to ensure the retirement income system remains fair,
sustainable, and trusted now and in the decades ahead.
Um, and the first message is that we are starting
actually from a strong position.
Our retirement savings system, uh,
which includes New Zealand Super Kiwi Saver
and other private savings, performs well
by international standards.
It provides broad coverage, has relatively low fiscal costs
and benefits from a younger
population than many other countries.
But as you know, the world in the policy
context is changing.
Um, historically,
new Zealand's performed well in reducing poverty
amongst people age 65 and over.
But this is changing. Uh,
financial vulnerability is increasing for over 60 fives.
44% of older New Zealanders feel exposed or poor.
The poverty rate for 65 plus has risen to 16.8%,
which is above the OECD average of 14.2.
Um, we know that housing costs
for the 65 plus are increasing.
Uh, mortgage free ownership, uh, fell from 83%
and 13% are still paying mortgages with 13% renting.
Um, as KiwiSaver continues to grow
in importance within the system,
existing disparities will probably continue to widen, um,
as retirement savings are in increments by factors such
as lifetime earnings and employment type.
Just so you know, men's kiwi saver balances are on average
25% higher than women's, uh,
and only 44% of, uh, the self employee contribute
to Kiwi Saver and they contribute half as much as, uh,
overall as employees.
So I think it's important I point this out
as these truths from our data
differ somewhat from the more common stereotypes
of older New Zealanders
and the supposed great wealth transfer.
In short, the wealth is only transferred to a few
and there are a few more problems emerging.
So what's happening next, which I think is
what this committee is going to be wrestling with.
So we've, we've thought hard about the demographic trends
over the next 50 years.
Um, and as you clearly know, New Zealand, um,
is experiencing a significant demographic shift.
The proportion of people aged over 65
and over is increasing rapidly driven
by longer life expectancies and declining birth rates.
By 2050, the number of people age 65 plus is projected
to increase by about 50%, while the number
of people under 40 will remain
roughly the same as it is today.
This is not a temporary baby boomer effect.
It is a sustained trend due
to sustained changes in birth rates and longevity.
So what are the implications for an aging population
for retirement and social services?
In short, one word, profound NZ super is projected
to remain a significant component of retirement income,
but the fiscal cost will, on a gross basis, rise from 54%,
sorry, 5.4% of GDP today
It is important to note that this is still
below the OECD average of 10.2%.
So we are still kind of in the middle.
Um, more people will be living longer in retirement
and the diversity of older New Zealand municipal increase
with greater variation in health, housing,
and financial circumstances, uh,
and home ownership amongst older people is declining.
And more people will enter the pension years with mortgages
or as renters, which increases the cost of financial stress
and material hardship.
Also to note, NZ super isn't designed
to cover significant housing costs.
You meant to be mortgage free
or being affordable public housing by that point.
So there are challenges
and opportunities, uh, facing the government of today
and governments of the future.
The system simplicity and universality of strengths,
but not all groups benefit equally, women, Maori Pacific,
peoples renters and the self-employed face greater risk
of hardship in in retirement wealth
and income inequality amongst older people are likely
to widen unless policy settings adapt
and the demand for age appropriate housing,
accessible healthcare and the
and community support will grow.
Um, every three years, a review is carried out
by the retirement commission.
This is your holiday reading.
Um, as required by law, it provides the government
with independent advice on
how retirement policies are performing
and what changes might be needed.
The review of retirement income policies 2025 was published
only last month, and it offers, uh, an opportunity
to take a system-wide view.
It looks across NZ super KiwiSaver
and private savings to understand how different parts
of the system interact.
So this joined up approach helps identify gaps, overlaps,
and opportunities for improvement.
This year's review ta uh,
includes targeted changes we can make now such
as improving KiwiSaver settings
and extending support through parental leave.
It also proposes reforms
to strengthen long term stewardship
and optimize areas for future thinking.
The, the most critical recommendation from the review is
to strengthen long term stewardship
of our retirement system.
We are proposing a 10 year retirement income roadmap guided
by a a, a cross party parliamentary working group,
and a Pan sector stewardship group led
by the retirement commission.
This approach is designed to most important,
build enduring political consensus
and stability across electoral cycles to ensure
that policy is joined up across NZ super KiwiSaver
and private savings and to enable regular monitoring,
public engagement.
Most important and evidence-based adjustment
as our population
and economy change, the worst thing that can happen
is piecemeal announcements
that don't look properly at the long term
and don't consider social impacts equity issues
and the need for long-term planning.
If you want to fiddle with New Zealand super, for instance,
then for heaven's sake, look at how
to make kiwi save better in the longer term,
but the right incentives for the right people.
At the same time. It's a system.
I often frame this as a women's issue as much as anything
because women have fewer retirement savings.
We live longer, and we disproportionately affected
by economic life shocks like divorce or business failure.
This is not just about fiscal issues, it's about the kind
of society we want to live in
and how we want to treat our older people.
So our work at the commission in, in,
in conclusion is guided by belief
that retirement income policies must be evidence-based
and shaped by long-term thinking.
The issues we face are not going away,
and it's crucial that our politicians work together on
enduring solutions.
We have time, this is a chance to pause, to take stock,
and to commit to a practical roadmap to the future, one
that extends beyond political cycles
and focuses on stability, dignity,
and mana in retirement.
So this is really important
because we can ensure that New Zealanders
not only have security in retirement today,
but that future generations, the ones paying
for current pensioners, can also look forward to a system
that is fair, sustainable, and trusted.
Um, I'm grateful that you are digging deep into these issues
because it needs to be done.
Uh, and we are here now to answer questions. Ana,
Uh, thank you very much for that.
Um, uh, very, very interesting, uh,
and a whole lot of issues there.

We can answer them.

Wednesday 17 December | Social Services and Community 
Briefing on New Zealand's ageing population
